title: Welcome to My Analytics Lab
text:
Hello!! My name is Ece Ozcan Karatop.
This is my personal webpage. I am a M.S. student in the Department of Industrial Engineering at Hacettepe University.
Please stay tuned to follow my works on data analytics, blog posts, and more.

title: Assignment 1
text:
My first assignment has four parts.


A brief summary of Mr. Vardar’s speech
In my view, Mr. Vardar’s presentation can be divided into three primary parts.
First, he stresses that Industrial Engineers possess the skills required to effectively leverage data science tools and emphasizes the strong connection between Industrial Engineering and data science practices. He presents data analytics as a valuable problem-solving tool for Industrial Engineers.
Second, Mr. Vardar points out that in designing complex systems, simplicity is often the most effective approach. Even when complex solutions are necessary, they should ideally begin with simple foundations.
Lastly, he refers to the Pareto Principle (the 80-20 rule), explaining that mastering just 20% of the skills in data analytics such as programming, data mining techniques, or using cloud platforms can satisfy roughly 80% of an Industrial Engineer’s practical needs.



Question: How can Industrial Engineers leverage data science tools to solve real-world problems, and why is understanding both fields important for career development?
Answer: Industrial Engineers can use data science tools to analyze complex datasets and optimize processes. Understanding both Industrial Engineering and data science allows engineers to bridge technical problem-solving skills with data-driven insights.
Multiple-Choice Question: According to Cem Vardar, which statement about the Pareto Principle (80-20 rule) in learning data analytics is correct?

Industrial Engineers need to learn 100% of data analytics skills to be effective.

Learning 20% of key data analytics skills can satisfy 80% of the practical needs.

Only programming skills matter; other tools are not necessary.

The Pareto Principle does not apply to Industrial Engineers.

Answer: B





Show Code
data(mtcars)
cars <- mtcars

compute_stats <- function(x) {
  list(
    mean = mean(x),
    median = median(x),
    variance = var(x),
    IQR = IQR(x),
    minimum = min(x),
    maximum = max(x)
  )
}

results <- list()

for (col_name in names(cars)) {
  results[[col_name]] <- compute_stats(cars[[col_name]])
  cat("\n============================\n")
  cat("Column:", col_name, "\n")
  print(results[[col_name]])
}

# Total number of NA values in the dataset
total_na <- sum(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))

cat("Total NA values in the dataset:", total_na, "\n")


Total NA values in the dataset: 11604 


Show Code
# Number of NA values per column
na_per_column <- colSums(is.na(polls_us_election_2016))

na_sorted <- sort(na_per_column, decreasing = TRUE)

head(na_sorted, 8)


rawpoll_mcmullin adjpoll_mcmullin  rawpoll_johnson  adjpoll_johnson 
            4178             4178             1409             1409 
           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 


Show Code
# Create a new object
polls_us_election_2016_new <- polls_us_election_2016

# Replace NA values
for (col_name in names(polls_us_election_2016_new)) {
  if (is.numeric(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])) {
    polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]][is.na(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])] <- my_birth_year + k
  } else if (is.character(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]]) || is.factor(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])) {
    polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]] <- as.character(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])
    polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]][is.na(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
}

cat("\nPreview of the new dataset after replacing NA values:\n")



Preview of the new dataset after replacing NA values:

title: Assignment 1
text:
In my view, Mr. Vardar’s presentation can be divided into three primary parts.
First, he stresses that Industrial Engineers possess the skills required to effectively leverage data science tools and emphasizes the strong connection between Industrial Engineering and data science practices. He presents data analytics as a valuable problem-solving tool for Industrial Engineers.
Second, Mr. Vardar points out that in designing complex systems, simplicity is often the most effective approach. Even when complex solutions are necessary, they should ideally begin with simple foundations.
Lastly, he refers to the Pareto Principle (the 80-20 rule), explaining that mastering just 20% of the skills in data analytics such as programming, data mining techniques, or using cloud platforms can satisfy roughly 80% of an Industrial Engineer’s practical needs.

title: Assignment 1
text:
Question: How can Industrial Engineers leverage data science tools to solve real-world problems, and why is understanding both fields important for career development?
Answer: Industrial Engineers can use data science tools to analyze complex datasets and optimize processes. Understanding both Industrial Engineering and data science allows engineers to bridge technical problem-solving skills with data-driven insights.
Multiple-Choice Question: According to Cem Vardar, which statement about the Pareto Principle (80-20 rule) in learning data analytics is correct?

Industrial Engineers need to learn 100% of data analytics skills to be effective.

Learning 20% of key data analytics skills can satisfy 80% of the practical needs.

Only programming skills matter; other tools are not necessary.

The Pareto Principle does not apply to Industrial Engineers.

Answer: B

Show Code
# Create a new object
polls_us_election_2016_new <- polls_us_election_2016

# Replace NA values
for (col_name in names(polls_us_election_2016_new)) {
  if (is.numeric(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])) {
    polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]][is.na(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])] <- my_birth_year + k
  } else if (is.character(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]]) || is.factor(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])) {
    polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]] <- as.character(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])
    polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]][is.na(polls_us_election_2016_new[[col_name]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
  }
}

cat("\nPreview of the new dataset after replacing NA values:\n")

# Display k rows

  new_selected_rows <- head(polls_us_election_2016_new, k)

} else {
  new_selected_rows <- tail(polls_us_election_2016_new, k)
  cat("\nk is odd, so the last", k, "rows of the new data frame are displayed.\n\n")
}
